text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy, carefully preserving the spleen and surrounding vessels during the procedure.
A thirty-one-year-old woman presented with a four-centimeter cystic mass that was incidentally discovered on right upper quadrant ultrasound during preoperative workup for bariatric surgery.
A liver retractor is placed through a right upper quadrant 5mm port to elevate the left lobe of the liver, providing better access to the surgical field for the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon decompresses the stomach by inserting a nasogastric tube, visibly reducing gastric distension.
The surgeon begins dissection of the gastrocolic ligament approximately six centimeters from the pylorus using a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues dissection along the major curvature of the stomach in a cephalad direction, gradually exposing the pancreas.
The surgeon begins dissection of the pancreas proximal to the cystic mass using a harmonic scalpel, focusing on the superior border of the pancreas while maintaining careful hemostasis.
The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery in combination with a harmonic scalpel to perform precise tissue dissection around the pancreatic region, focusing on the area proximal to the cystic mass.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the splenic artery using a Maryland dissector and hook electrocautery, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a red vessel loop to encircle the splenic artery, securing proximal control and providing traction for subsequent vessel dissection.
The surgeon secures the red vessel loop around the splenic artery with a 5mm endo clip, ensuring proximal control of the vessel.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the inferior border of the pancreas using a harmonic scalpel, maintaining careful control over the tissue to ensure hemostasis.
The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery to divide the connective tissue attachments at the posterior aspect of the pancreas, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon elevates the pancreas anteriorly and inserts a Maryland dissector behind it, approaching the confluence of the portal vein to continue dissection at the pancreas's superior border.
The surgeon uses a medium-sized Penrose drain to encircle and elevate the pancreas, preparing the area for pancreatic transection.
The surgeon inserts a laparoscopic linear stapler with tissue reinforcement material, positioning it by elevating the pancreas using the Penrose drain.
The surgeon transects the pancreas, achieving good hemostasis at the cut edges, with visible control of bleeding.

The surgeon performs a careful dissection along the tortuous splenic artery and vein using a combination of hook electrocautery and harmonic scalpel, meticulously separating these vessels from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a red vessel loop to apply traction while dissecting the splenic artery away from the pancreas parenchyma.
The surgeon continues dissection toward the splenic hilum, progressively detaching the specimen from surrounding tissues until it is completely separated.
The surgeon displays the intact splenic vessels, highlighting their preservation following the dissection and transection of the pancreas.
The dissected specimen, including the pancreas and associated structures, is displayed following the completion of the transection and dissection process.
The operative time was approximately four hours, with an estimated blood loss of less than one hundred milliliters.
The patient made an uneventful recovery and was discharged home on post-operative day three.
Histopathology examination revealed a three point five centimeter mucinous cystic neoplasm with low-grade dysplasia.
Proximal and distal margins are free of lesion.
